elements.sent-2411-001: There is widespread concern about the negative impacts of social media feed ranking algorithms on political polarization.
elements.sent-2411-002: Leveraging advancements in large language models (LLMs), we develop an approach to re-rank feeds in real-time to test the effects of content that is likely to polarize: expressions of antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity (AAPA).
elements.sent-2411-003: In a preregistered 10-day field experiment on X/Twitter with 1,256 consented participants, we increase or decrease participants' exposure to AAPA in their algorithmically curated feeds.
elements.sent-2411-004: We observe more positive outparty feelings when AAPA exposure is decreased and more negative outparty feelings when AAPA exposure is increased.
elements.sent-2411-005: Exposure to AAPA content also results in an immediate increase in negative emotions, such as sadness and anger.
elements.sent-2411-006: The interventions do not significantly impact traditional engagement metrics such as re-post and favorite rates.
elements.sent-2411-007: These findings highlight a potential pathway for developing feed algorithms that mitigate affective polarization by addressing content that undermines the shared values required for a healthy democracy.
elements.sent-2411-008: Social media algorithms profoundly impact our lives: they curate what we see~\cite{bakshy2015exposure in ways that can shape our opinions~\cite{brady2023overperception,lee2018does,feezell2021exploring , our moods~\cite{kramer2014experimental,brady2021social,allcott2020welfare , and our actions~\cite{bond201261,tufekci2017twitter,jost2018social,smith2023digital,zuckerberg2021us .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Due to the power that these ranking algorithms have to direct our attention, many have expressed concern that they may be exacerbating negative societal outcomes~\cite{rose2023outrage,lorenz2023systematic,tucker2018social,oldemburgo2024twitter,beam2020facebook .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Of particular concern is whether feed algorithms cause affective polarization---hostility toward opposing political parties~\cite{iyengar2019origins,boxell2024cross,iyengar2018strengthening .}
elements.sent-2411-011: If social media algorithms are causing affective polarization, they might not only bear responsibility for rising political incivility online~\cite{frimer2023incivility but also pose a risk to trust in democratic institutions~\cite{druckman2023does .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this case, isolating the algorithmic design choices that cause polarization could offer alternative algorithmic approaches.
elements.sent-2411-013: Research investigating this question has so far yielded mixed results due to limited collaboration between platforms and researchers, frequent changes in feed-ranking algorithms, and the lack of strong incentives for platforms to address the issue~\cite{haidt_bail_social_media .}
elements.sent-2411-014: A major hypothesized mechanism for how feed algorithms cause polarization is a self-reinforcing engagement loop: users engage with content aligning with their political views, the feed algorithm interprets this engagement as a positive signal, and the algorithm exposes even more politically aligned content to users, leading to a polarizing cycle.
elements.sent-2411-015: Some studies support this hypothesis, finding that online interactions exacerbate polarization~\cite{barnidge2017exposure , potentially because of the increased visibility of hostile political discussions~\cite{bor2022psychology , divisive language~\cite{banks2021polarizedfeeds,rathje2021out,van2021social,suhay2018polarizing,kim2021distorting , and content that reinforces existing beliefs~\cite{cho2020search .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, large-scale field experiments aimed at reducing polarization by intervening on the feed algorithm, for example by increasing exposure to out-party content, have found both a decrease~\cite{levy2021social and increase~\cite{bail2018exposure in polarization.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Similarly, recent large-scale experiments on Facebook and Instagram found no evidence that exposure to in-party sources or a simpler reverse-chronological algorithm affected polarization and political attitudes~\cite{guess2023social,nyhan2023like during the 2020 U.S.. election~\cite{bagchi_2024_13787981 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These mixed results have led to difficulty in identifying what, if any, algorithmic intervention might help.
elements.sent-2411-019: We can distill the goals of these prior interventions to an alternative hypothesis: that feed algorithms cause affective polarization by exposing us to content that polarizes.
elements.sent-2411-020: An algorithm that upranks genuine political dialogue is less likely to polarize than an algorithm that upranks demagoguery.
elements.sent-2411-021: This hypothesis has been difficult to operationalize into interventions, making studies that intervene on cross-partisan exposure and reverse-chronological ranking attractive but more diffuse in their impact and thus more likely to observe mixed results.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, recent advances in AI large language models (LLMs) now provide an opportunity to create a ranking intervention that more directly targets the focal hypothesis~\cite{bernstein2023embedding .}
elements.sent-2411-023: We draw in particular on a recent large-scale field experiment that articulated eight categories of antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity as bipartisan threats to the healthy functioning of democracy~\cite{voelkel2024megastudy .}
elements.sent-2411-024: We operationalize these eight categories into an AI classifier that labels expressions of these constructs in social media posts, does so as accurately as trained annotators agree with each other, and produces the desired depolarization effects in a lab setting on a fixed feed~\cite{jia2024embedding .}
elements.sent-2411-025: This real-time classification enables us to perform a scalable, content-based, real-time reranking experiment on participants' own feeds in the field~\cite{piccardi2024reranking .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We conduct a preregistered field experiment on X (Twitter), the most used platform for political discourse in the US~\cite{mcclain2024americans , that re-ranks participants' social media content by either increasing or decreasing exposure to content that expresses these eight factors of antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity (AAPA).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Consented participants \Secref{sec:ethics installed a web extension and experienced this intervention through a real-time reranking of their feeds when they visited the X website over the course of a week.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We measured the intervention's effect on affective polarization~\cite{iyengar2012affect and emotional experience~\cite{russell1980circumplex .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Compared to control conditions that did not rerank the feed, increased AAPA exposure in the ranking algorithm led to colder feelings toward the political outgroup, whereas decreased AAPA exposure led to warmer feelings.
elements.sent-2411-030: These changes also had an effect on participants' levels of negative emotions---anger and sadness---as measured through in-feed surveys.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our intervention does not lead to statistically significant changes to traditional engagement metrics.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, because we observe higher engagement on political social media content than other content, the engagement-prioritizing nature of current feed algorithms is likely already amplifying AAPA content.
elements.sent-2411-033: This work demonstrates not just causal evidence of feed algorithm polarization but a potential intervention to mitigate it by downranking content that violates shared democratic values.
elements.section-2411-002: Exposure to Antidemocratic Attitudes and Partisan Animosity
elements.sent-2411-034: There are many viable theories as to which content, if amplified, could impact affective polarization.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this experiment, we draw specifically on prior work that articulates eight factors as problematic outcomes for healthy democratic functioning, referred to as ``antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity''~\cite{voelkel2024megastudy : (1)~partisan animosity, (2)~support for undemocratic practices, (3)~support for partisan violence, (4)~support for undemocratic candidates, (5)~opposition to bipartisanship, (6)~social distrust, (7)~social distance, and (8)~biased evaluation of politicized facts \Appref{sup:poda .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We refer to these eight factors collectively as AAPA (for Antidemocratic Attitudes and Partisan Animosity), and we hypothesize that increased or decreased exposure to AAPA content will cause corresponding changes to affective polarization.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our feed ranking intervention first identifies only political content in the feed, using a broad definition from the Pew Research Center~\cite{bestvater2022politics that includes mentions of officials and activists, social issues, news, and current events \Appref{sup:political_classifier .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: We then score each political post from zero to eight based on the number of the eight AAPA factors expressed in its text, and we classify a post as AAPA if it reflects at least four of these eight factors.
elements.sent-2411-039: This scoring is performed by a large language model~\Appref{sup:scoring , which shows a high correlation with human judgment on identifying these eight factors in social media posts~\cite{jia2024embedding .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/main_diagram.pdf} \caption{Overview of the field experiment on X. (A) Participants complete a pre-experiment survey to rate their feelings toward the opposing party and report their emotions. Then, they are randomly assigned to one of the two parallel experiments, increased or reduced AAPA content exposure, and further randomized into treatment and control groups. After 10 days, they complete a post-experiment survey. (B) Timeline of the experiment, which spans ten days. During the first three days, no intervention is applied to measure the participants' baseline responses. After day three, participants in the treatment groups received increased or decreased AAPA exposure. In-feed surveys are occasionally shown throughout the baseline and the intervention period. (C) The interventions downranked or upranked AAPA posts in the participants' feeds, depending on their treatment assignment. In-feed surveys are occasionally added to the feed to capture participants' in-feed responses.}\label{fig:experiment}
elements.sent-2411-040: We designed two parallel experiments to measure the impact of reducing and increasing exposure to AAPA posts (\Figref{fig:experiment ).}
elements.sent-2411-041: The first, a ``Reduced Exposure'' experiment, downranked all AAPA content, pushing it lower in their feeds.
elements.sent-2411-042: In contrast, the second, an ``Increased Exposure'' experiment, upranked attitudinally-aligned AAPA posts into participants' feeds.
elements.sent-2411-043: Both experiments are randomized controlled trials.
elements.sent-2411-044: After providing informed consent and installing our web extension, participants were randomly assigned to one of the two experiments and then randomly divided into treatment or control conditions for each experiment.
elements.sent-2411-045: The overall experiment spanned ten days, with the first three days serving as a baseline period, during which no intervention was applied.
elements.sent-2411-046: During the subsequent seven days, participants in the treatment groups received the modified feed (downranked or upranked AAPA posts), while those in the control groups continued to receive the original, unmodified feed.
elements.sent-2411-047: We assess outcomes at two different time points: post-experiment and in-feed.
elements.sent-2411-048: To measure post-experiment effects, we administered a survey asking participants how warm they feel towards people of the opposing party (0--100 degrees on a feeling thermometer) and about the emotions that they associate with reading social media over the previous week (5-point scale between ``never'' and ``all the time'').
elements.sent-2411-049: We surveyed the participants both before and after the experiment, enabling us to quantify the impact of the intervention at the end of the week-long treatment \Secref{method:survey .}
elements.sent-2411-050: To measure in-feed effects, we added posts containing a small survey directly in the participant's feed.
elements.sent-2411-051: These surveys, modeled on the ecological momentary assessment approach~\cite{shiffman2008ecological , resemble typical social media posts but are colored to stand out and contain a slider survey instrument.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Each survey randomly selects one of two outcomes to assess: (1)~affective polarization, where participants rate their feelings toward the opposing party, or (2)~emotional responses, where participants report the extent to which they feel two emotions, one randomly selected positive emotion (excited or calm), and one randomly selected negative emotion (angry or sad).
elements.sent-2411-053: The in-feed surveys appeared with a decreasing probability after each feed intervention (post downranking or upranking) and were shown at an equivalent rate and position in the control conditions \Secref{method:survey .}
elements.sent-2411-054: To administer the interventions, we developed a browser extension for Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge that reranks participants' feeds in real time~\cite{piccardi2024reranking .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The extension activates when participants access the ``For You'' feed on X.
elements.sent-2411-056: It intercepts the participants' original feed and sends it to a remote backend, which scores and reranks the posts \Appref{sup:platform .}
elements.sent-2411-057: This process adds a latency of about three seconds when opening the feed for the first time, which is consistent across all experimental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-058: To rerank the original feeds, we first identify political posts~\Appref{sup:political_classifier and then determine whether those posts are classified as AAPA.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In the Reduced Exposure treatment condition, we downrank all AAPA posts in the participants' feeds.
elements.sent-2411-060: If participants continue to scroll down far enough, e.g., across several incremental loads of more content, these demoted posts will appear (\Figref{fig:seen_by_day_reduced ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: Before downranking all the AAPA posts, we randomly select the position of one AAPA post and, if the in-feed survey is sampled to be included, insert the post with the survey in the next available position \Appref{sup:reduced .}
elements.sent-2411-062: This survey position is consistent across treatment and control conditions.
elements.sent-2411-063: Thus, participants in the control condition will see AAPA posts and the in-feed survey, while participants in the treatment condition will see the survey in the position where the first AAPA post used to be, but all AAPA posts are placed further down.
elements.sent-2411-064: The survey is limited to only a few times per day to avoid fatigue \Secref{method:survey .}
elements.sent-2411-065: In the Increased Exposure experiment, since we lack access to the platform's full post inventory, we sourced AAPA posts from the inventory of all posts in the ``For You'' feeds of all other study participants.
elements.sent-2411-066: We selected a random position in the original feed and upranked a random AAPA post to that position.
elements.sent-2411-067: The post containing the survey, if included, is inserted in the next available position.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the control condition, the survey is still added in the randomly sampled position, but no post is upranked.
elements.sent-2411-069: As in the Reduced Exposure experiment, the survey is placed consistently in the treatment and control conditions.
elements.sent-2411-070: During continuous scrolling sessions, this process repeats approximately every 30 posts \Appref{sup:increased .}
elements.sent-2411-071: The Reduced Exposure treatment downranked 85.4 AAPA posts on average (median = 22) per user per day, and the Increased Exposure upranked 10.9 AAPA posts on average (median = 5.43) per user per day.
elements.sent-2411-072: We recruited participants from two online survey platforms, CloudResearch and Bovitz-Forthright~\cite{stagnaro2024representativeness .}
elements.sent-2411-073: We targeted participants who use X, are 18 or older, reside in the United States, and self-identify as either Democrats or Republicans \Appref{sup:participants .}
elements.sent-2411-074: After obtaining informed consent, participants installed our web extension.
elements.sent-2411-075: To ensure the participants had enough political content, we used a pre-screening task to filter for users whose ``For You'' feeds contained at least 5\% of posts related to politics or social issues.
elements.sent-2411-076: The participants were recruited during the U.S.
elements.sent-2411-077: We preregistered a target of at least 1,100 participants to complete the full study, broken down as 500 in the Increased Exposure experiment and 600 in the Reduced Exposure experiment based on power analyses performed on pilot studies.
elements.sent-2411-078: Toward this goal, a total of 1,662 participants completed the initial survey and qualified for inclusion in the study (\Figref{fig:study_flow ).}
elements.sent-2411-079: Participants were randomly assigned to either the Increased or Reduced Exposure group until the preregistered experiment-specific quotas were reached~\cite{piccardi_saveski_jia_hancock_tsai_bernstein_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Of the qualified participants, N=1,256 completed the full study by responding to follow-up surveys the day after the end of the study: 727 in the Reduced Exposure experiment and 529 in the Increased Exposure experiment.
elements.sent-2411-081: Both treatment and control groups were well balanced across covariates, and there was no evidence of selective attrition between conditions \Secref{sec:experimental-design .}
elements.sent-2411-082: On average, participants viewed 158 posts per day (median = 94) and spent 43.8 minutes (median = 19.2 minutes) per day with a browser tab in the foreground on their X feed.
elements.sent-2411-083: During the baseline period, an average of 32\% of the posts participants viewed were related to politics or social issues.
elements.sent-2411-084: Of these, 33.1\% were classified as AAPA, which comprised an average of 10.6\% of the total feed content.
elements.sent-2411-085: The interventions had the intended effects (\Figref{fig:feed_scores ).}
elements.sent-2411-086: During the baseline period, participants across all groups were exposed to political posts with similar average AAPA scores.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, after the intervention began, participants in the treatment condition of the Reduced Exposure experiment experienced a noticeable decrease in exposure to such posts (-91.2\% compared to control), while those in the treatment condition of the Increased Exposure experiment were exposed to more AAPA posts (+23.1\% compared to control), resulting in higher average scores.
elements.sent-2411-088: Over the full ten days of the experiment, participants answered an average of 13.8 (median = 11) in-feed affective polarization questions in the Increased Exposure experiment and 12.6 (median = 9) in the Reduced Exposure experiment.
elements.sent-2411-089: Similarly, they answered an average of 14.0 (median = 11) emotion surveys in the Increased Exposure experiment and 12.5 (median = 9) in the Reduced Exposure experiment--each survey requiring ratings for two emotions.
elements.sent-2411-090: In the post-survey, we asked participants whether the browser extension had impacted their experience on X.
elements.sent-2411-091: Across conditions, most participants reported that they did not notice any impact in their online experience (74.2\%), while others guessed changes that were not part of the intervention~\Appref{sup:impact .}
elements.sent-2411-092: These results indicate a limited understanding of the experimental feed ranking intervention and suggest that it is less likely that demand effects might be influencing the outcomes.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/main_affective_polarization.pdf} \caption{Effects of reducing and increasing exposure to AAPA content in participants’ feeds on their feeling towards the out-party (feeling thermometer scale between 0: cold, and 100: warm) relative to the corresponding control group. Participants were surveyed within the feed during the intervention and off-platform after the experiment. The error bars represent 95\% confidence intervals.} \label{fig:results_polarization}
elements.sent-2411-093: Exposure to AAPA significantly influenced affective polarization (\Figref{fig:results_polarization ).}
elements.sent-2411-094: Participants in the reduced AAPA exposure experiment reported a significant post-experiment effect, corresponding to an outparty warmth increase of 2.11 degrees (95\% CIs: [0.15, 4.06]; P=0.035).
elements.sent-2411-095: Conversely, participants in the Increased Exposure experiment exhibited a symmetric reduction in affective warmth toward the opposing party of -2.48 degrees (95\% CIs: [-4.79, -0.17]; P=0.036).
elements.sent-2411-096: We did not observe any significant heterogeneity in treatment effects across the preregistered moderators~\Appref{sup:heterogeneous , including party identification, indicating that the effect was consistent and bipartisan.}
elements.sent-2411-097: As is common~\cite{santoro2022promise , we contextualize these effects against the rising levels of partisan animosity in American society over time: our effects correspond to a decrease and increase in affective polarization equivalent to 3 and 3.6 years, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-098: We observed even stronger effects with the in-feed assessment, with Reduced Exposure causing an increase of 3.24 degrees (95\% CIs: [1.21, 5.27]; P=0.002) and Increased Exposure causing a cooling of -2.56 degrees (95\% CIs: [-4.53, -0.59]; P=0.011).
elements.sent-2411-099: In an exploratory analysis, we investigated which AAPA factors were predictive of changes in affective polarization.
elements.sent-2411-100: Specifically, a higher proportion of posts supporting Partisan Animosity (95\% CIs: [-13.49, -2.61]; P=0.003), Opposition to Bipartisan Cooperation (95\% CIs: [-26.55, -4.40]; P=0.006), and Biased Evaluation of Politicized Facts (95\% CIs: [-12.83, -2.21]; P=0.005;) predicted a colder feeling toward the opposing party by the end of the study (\Figref{fig:factors_contribution ).}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/main_emotions.pdf} \caption{ Effects of reducing and increasing exposure to AAPA content in participants’ feeds on their experiences of emotion (in-feed scale ranged from 0: ``none at all'' to 100: ``extremely'', post-experiment scale ranged from 1: ``never'' to 5: ``all the time'') relative to the corresponding control group. Participants were surveyed within the feed during the intervention and off-platform after the experiment. The filled circles represent statistical significance ($P<0.05$, adjusted for multiple hypothesis testing), and the error bars represent 95\% confidence intervals. } \label{fig:results_emotions}
elements.sent-2411-101: Changes to the feed algorithm caused in-feed, but not post-experiment, changes to participants' negative emotions (\Figref{fig:results_emotions ).}
elements.sent-2411-102: Reduced Exposure caused a change of -5.05 points (95\% CIs: [-7.68, -2.42]; $P_{adj}$=0.002) for ``angry'' and -3.68 points (95\% CIs: [-6.42, -0.94]; $P_{adj}$=0.024) for ``sad.'' Likewise, in the Increased Exposure, participants report a symmetric increase in negative emotions: 5.13 points (95\% CIs: [2.05, 8.22]; $P_{adj}$=0.013) for ``angry'' and 4.38 points (95\% CIs: [1.00, 7.76]; $P_{adj}$=0.036) for ``sad.'' Neither intervention had an effect on the positive emotions ``calm'' and ``excited.'' Post-experiment emotion effects were not significant, suggesting that the effects were likely short-lived.
elements.sent-2411-103: We tested whether our interventions impacted platform engagement \Appref{sup:engagement .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Neither experiment observed imbalances in attrition rates across conditions.
elements.sent-2411-105: Moreover, in both experiments, the treatment had no measurable impact on daily sessions---Increased Exposure: 1.65 control vs. 1.53 treatment, n.s.; Reduced Exposure: 1.53 vs. 1.49, n.s.
elements.sent-2411-106: Likewise, there was no significant effect on reshare rates---Increased Exposure: 0.52\% vs. 1.07\%, n.s.; decreased exposure: 0.74\% vs. 0.46\%, n.s.
elements.sent-2411-107: In addition, there was no effect on favorite rates---Increased Exposure: 4.48\% vs. 5.46\%, n.s; decreased exposure: 4.96\% vs. 4.15\%, n.s.
elements.sent-2411-108: We also observed that the differences between treatment and control groups in terms of the number of new posts, replies, and time spent on X during the experimental period were not statistically significant.
elements.sent-2411-109: These null results persisted when controlling for baseline behavior.
elements.sent-2411-110: While nominally consistently in the expected direction, none of the statistical tests were significant.
elements.sent-2411-111: It is possible that an even larger study could observe differences; however, our data suggests that the magnitude of any effect would be small.
elements.sent-2411-112: In an exploratory analysis, we did observe that political content had higher engagement than non-political content, confirming the findings of previous work~\cite{guess2023reshares .}
elements.sent-2411-113: When focusing only on political content, we found that AAPA posts do not get more engagement than moderate political posts \Appref{sup:engagement .}
elements.sent-2411-114: In this study, we conducted a field experiment to investigate the effects of feed ranking algorithms that decrease or increase exposure to contact that polarizes.
elements.sent-2411-115: Our findings demonstrate that repeated exposure to AAPA posts, particularly during heightened political tensions such as months before the US election, significantly influences users' affective polarization and emotional responses.
elements.sent-2411-116: A feed ranking algorithm that controls exposure to such content produces measurable changes, equivalent to mitigating approximately three years of affective polarization, even after just one week of treatment.
elements.sent-2411-117: Importantly, our results indicate that the intervention does not disproportionately impact any specific political leaning or demographic group, suggesting that the approach is bipartisan.
elements.sent-2411-118: The success of this method shows that it can be integrated into social media AIs to mitigate harmful societal consequences.
elements.sent-2411-119: We also present evidence that this impact can likely be achieved with a limited effect on business metrics like engagement.
elements.sent-2411-120: Limitations of our study include a sample of partisan US users restricted to the web version of X during a specific period, which may affect the generalizability of the findings~\cite{budak2024misunderstanding,eckles2022algorithmic .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Our population is limited to X users with at least 5\% of political content (73.4\% of the screened), but we intentionally selected this group to ensure the interventions can make a difference.
elements.sent-2411-122: Additionally, the experiment examines short-term effects—immediate in-feed responses and outcomes measured the day after the experiment---but does not address whether these effects persist over time.
elements.sent-2411-123: However, in scenarios where this ranking approach is integrated into the feed algorithm, the intervention would be continuously applied.
elements.sent-2411-124: Our reliance on in-feed surveys to assess immediate responses to stimuli follows established practices of ecological momentary assessment (EMA)~\cite{shiffman2008ecological .}
elements.sent-2411-125: To mitigate the risk of demand characteristics associated with the close proximity of the assessment, we followed best practices in designing the experiment~\cite{zizzo2010experimenter , providing only essential information about the study's purpose.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Post-survey responses to an open-ended question about their experience during the study suggest that these precautions were effective, as participants did not report any impact on their experience attributable to their assigned condition.
elements.sent-2411-127: Describing the causal relation between social media and polarization as a unified phenomenon, of course, remains challenging: platforms are complex socio-technical systems, and small variations in design or exogenous factors can lead to different outcomes~\cite{yarchi2021political .}
elements.sent-2411-128: Future research should explore whether these effects persist over longer periods or in less politically charged contexts and examine the efficacy of feed reranking across different platforms and cultural settings.
elements.sent-2411-129: As political polarization and societal division become increasingly linked to social media activity, our findings provide a potential pathway for platforms to address these challenges through algorithmic interventions.
elements.sent-2411-130: By embedding pro-democracy attitudes into AI-driven feeds~\cite{bernstein2023embedding,jia2024embedding , we offer a concrete solution that could contribute to healthier democratic discourse, enhance social trust, and help mitigate the risks of deepening polarization.}
elements.sent-2411-131: We recruited participants from two online platforms, Bovitz and CloudResearch.
elements.sent-2411-132: Using platform-specific filters, we targeted individuals who met the following criteria: U.S.
elements.sent-2411-133: Participants were invited to complete a two-minute screening task to assess eligibility for the study.
elements.sent-2411-134: The experiment was restricted to desktop users on Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge browsers.
elements.sent-2411-135: Upon accepting the consent form, participants were instructed to install a web extension that launched X in a new browser window, automatically scrolled through their ``For You'' feed to capture approximately 180 posts, and calculated the proportion of posts related to politics or social issues using our classifier \Appref{sup:political_classifier .}
elements.sent-2411-136: Only participants with at least 5\% of posts related to politics or social issues were invited to join the study, ensuring that participants regularly engaged with political content, a necessary condition for testing the intervention.
elements.sent-2411-137: Participants were compensated \$0.50 for completing the screening task and \$20 upon finishing the study.
elements.sent-2411-138: On Bovitz, 4,293 participants initiated the screening task, and 1,753 successfully installed the extension and accessed X.
elements.sent-2411-139: The observed drop in participation was primarily due to the use of unsupported devices or browsers (e.g., mobile).
elements.sent-2411-140: Participants who collected fewer than 100 posts due to technical issues (e.g., conflicts with other browser extensions) or who deliberately interrupted the screening were excluded from the study, leaving 1,688 eligible participants.
elements.sent-2411-141: After applying the 5\% political content filter, 1,373 participants were invited to continue with the study, of which 1,155 completed the pre-survey and were successfully enrolled.
elements.sent-2411-142: Of these, 720 were randomly assigned to the Reduced Exposure experiment and 435 to the Increased Exposure experiment, as our pilot study revealed the first group required more participants to detect the effect of the intervention~\cite{piccardi_saveski_jia_hancock_tsai_bernstein_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-143: On CloudResearch, 1,333 participants began the screening, and 1,036 successfully installed the extension and accessed X.
elements.sent-2411-144: The lower dropout rate compared to Bovitz is attributed to the platform’s use of more specific filters, such as restricting participation to desktop users and highlighting the need for software installation, as well as advanced filters targeting active X users.
elements.sent-2411-145: After accounting for successful data collection (998 participants) and applying the 5\% political content criterion, 604 participants were invited to the study.
elements.sent-2411-146: Among these, 508 participants joined, with 233 in the Increased Exposure experiment and 275 in the Reduced Exposure experiment.
elements.sent-2411-147: Participants were invited to engage daily and received reminders via email.
elements.sent-2411-148: After ten days, participants with minimal participation--those for who we recorded less than ten feed load events--were excluded from the study and did not receive the post-survey.
elements.sent-2411-149: The final completion rates were 69\% (798) for Bovitz and 90\% (458) for CloudResearch.
elements.sent-2411-150: Of the participants who completed the study, 602 (47.9\%) identified as female, 631 (50.2\%) as male, and 23 (1.9\%) as other gender identities.
elements.sent-2411-151: In terms of political affiliation, 831 (66.1\%) were Democrats, and 425 (33.9\%) were Republicans.

elements.sent-2411-218: We define AAPA based on eight variables identified~\cite{voelkel2024megastudy as potentially harmful to democratic functioning.}
elements.sent-2411-219: While the previous study tested multiple interventions to measure the impact on these variables, we focus on content expressing support for these outcomes, following findings from an in-lab experiment~\cite{jia2024embedding .}
elements.sent-2411-221: Partisan animosity (v1): defined as dislike for opposing partisans~\cite{iyengar2019origins .}
elements.sent-2411-222: Support for undemocratic practices (v2): defined as willingness to forgo democratic principles for partisan gain~\cite{levitsky2019democracies,svolik2018polarization.}
elements.sent-2411-223: Support for partisan violence (v3): defined as willingness to use violent tactics against outpartisans~\cite{kalmoe2022radical.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Support for undemocratic candidates (v4): defined as willingness to ignore democratic practices to elect inparty candidates \cite{graham2020democracy.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Opposition to bipartisan cooperation (v5): defined as resistance to cross-partisan collaboration \cite{hetherington2020washington.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Social distrust (v6): defined as distrust of people in general~\cite{putnam2000bowling.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Social distance (v7): defined as resistance to interpersonal contact with outpartisans \cite{bishop2009big.}
elements.sent-2411-228: Biased evaluation of politicized facts (v8): defined as skepticism of facts that favor the worldview of the other party~\cite{nyhan2010corrections.}
elements.sent-2411-229: These definitions, evaluated in previous work~\cite{jia2024embedding , were used to prompt the LLM (see \Tabref{tab:prompts ).}}

elements.sent-2411-234: We developed a browser extension to run the field experiment intervening in real-time in the participants' original feeds~\cite{piccardi_saveski_jia_hancock_tsai_bernstein_2024 .}

elements.sent-2411-274: We developed a custom experimental platform focused on performance to ensure the experiment introduces minimal disruption to the user experience on X.
elements.sent-2411-275: Our implementation consists of a browser extension and a REST backend deployed as a cloud service.
elements.sent-2411-276: The browser extension handles editing the X feed and managing all interactions with the participants, including the onboarding steps.
elements.sent-2411-277: It is compatible with Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge and requires only storage permissions to save the user identifier.
elements.sent-2411-278: The extension manages the ranking manipulation by modifying the content available on the X feed in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-279: Our implementation allows us to inject custom code on the main page to override the methods of the native XMLHttpRequest object.
elements.sent-2411-280: This approach allows us to intercept the communication with the server and modify the execution flow of the requests.
elements.sent-2411-281: Our browser extension subscribes to the requests endpoints used to retrieve the new content to add to the feed, interrupt the execution flow, send the server response to our backend for scoring and reranking, and finally restore the execution flow by calling the original methods with the response customized by our server.
elements.sent-2411-282: This approach requires careful understanding of the communication protocol with the server, but it offers significant advantages: first, the content can be modified by editing structured JSON objects without manipulating the final HTML, and second, by selecting what requests to intercept, it allows logging the interactions with the web page tracked by X without adding custom listeners.
elements.sent-2411-283: Additionally, the browser extension interacts with the main page by editing the DOM to add visual elements.
elements.sent-2411-284: These widgets include the in-feed surveys that are added to the feed as regular posts and a top fixed banner used to notify the participant about possible registration errors or that the experiment is completed, with the invitation to take the final survey and remove the extension.
elements.sent-2411-285: Finally, the extension manages the onboarding flow to ensure that participants can register and join the study only by following links that automatically guide them in installing the extension and setting the identifier from the recruitment platform~\cite{piccardi2024reranking .}
elements.sent-2411-286: The backend is responsible for reranking the feed, logging the client events, sending daily participation reminders by email, and orchestrating the participants’ registration.
elements.sent-2411-287: The server is written in Python and Java using MongoDB and Memcached for storage and caching.
elements.sent-2411-288: When the browser extension interrupts the execution flow to request the reranking of the feed, X's rotating circular loading indicator is extended until our reranking is complete.
elements.sent-2411-289: The implementation choices are guided by minimizing the response time to ensure minimal destruction of the user experience.
elements.sent-2411-290: The feed reranking proceeds in four steps: first, the response from X’s server, typically containing 35 posts, is sent to our backend and parsed to extract the text from the posts.
elements.sent-2411-291: The implementations focused on individual posts and in-feed conversations, ignoring the other types of content, such as ads or users’ recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-292: Second, all the posts are evaluated by a political classifier \Appref{sup:political_classifier to filter the posts that must be scored for the presence of the eight factors problematic for democratic functioning.}
elements.sent-2411-293: This service runs as a standalone Python web service, accelerated by GPU, that introduces, on average, a latency of 490 ms (Std = 111 ms).
elements.sent-2411-294: Then, the political posts are dispatched to a Java server that manages an HTTP connection pool with OpenAI APIs.
elements.sent-2411-295: The service batches the posts to reduce the number of requests and uses a pool of tokens to minimize latency and avoid the rate limit exceptions.
elements.sent-2411-296: Finally, the scores are parsed and returned to the main backend, which applies the downranking intervention and adds the in-feed surveys.
elements.sent-2411-297: These steps are replicated in the same way in the control condition, but in that case, the backend returns the original feed.
elements.sent-2411-298: The downranked posts are saved in a cache (Memcached) and added to the feed when the user reaches the new position in a continuous scrolling session.
elements.sent-2411-299: The backend also stores the logs of interactions with the feed.
elements.sent-2411-300: When a participant interacts with the feed on X, the extension regularly shares with the backend what actions are performed on the main page.
elements.sent-2411-301: This data is limited to the actions on the main feed and does not include any private content.
elements.sent-2411-302: The actions recorded include when a post enters the main viewport, likes, favorites, reposts, clicks on external links, and the time spent on the browser tab.
elements.sent-2411-303: To facilitate communication with the participants in the registration flow, they are invited to share an email address.
elements.sent-2411-304: The platform uses this address to send automatic messages that guide the participant in completing the study successfully.
elements.sent-2411-305: These messages include a copy of the onboarding instructions and the consent form for future reference, a daily reminder sent at 6 PM (participant's local time) inviting the participant to check their X feed, and the end-of-study notification with the link to the post-survey and the instruction to remove the browser extension.
elements.sent-2411-306: Finally, the backend orchestrates the registration flow of the participants~\cite{piccardi_saveski_jia_hancock_tsai_bernstein_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-307: Since the browser extension does not support passing custom installation parameters, the backend is responsible for guiding the onboarding flow to configure the extension for the current participant.
elements.sent-2411-308: After the termination of the pre-survey, the participants are redirected to our web server using a URL that encodes all the parameters relevant for the extension to work, such as the identifier and political position.
elements.sent-2411-309: Our server adds a browser cookie with this data and displays the step-by-step instructions for installing the extension.
elements.sent-2411-310: Participants are then redirected to the Chrome store, where they install the browser extension.
elements.sent-2411-311: As soon as the extension is installed, it opens a browser tab and loads to a welcome page hosted on our coordination server, where it has access to the cookies information previously set.

elements.sent-2411-314: Since our downranking intervention applies only to political posts, we developed a classifier to prefilter the relevant posts.
elements.sent-2411-315: Since GPT has a pay-per-use business model and calling an external API service call can introduce additional latency, we ensure that GPT is used to score only the content in which the eight variables are relevant.
elements.sent-2411-316: The political classifier is a distilled version of GPT-4 by fine-tuning a RoBERTa model to identify political posts, including mentions of officials and activists, social issues, news, and current events.
elements.sent-2411-317: The posts used to train the model were collected with the Twitter API in July 2023.
elements.sent-2411-318: To select the posts, we replicated the reverse chronological feeds of users across the political spectrum for two weeks and took a random sample of 50,000 posts.
elements.sent-2411-319: We first selected 300 users and then collected the posts by the users they followed.
elements.sent-2411-320: To select the users, we first obtained the followers of all members of Congress.
elements.sent-2411-321: Then, we categorized each user in the superset of followers based on how many Republican and Democrat members of Congress they followed: (a) far-left: followed 4-6 Democrats and no Republicans; (b) left: followed 1-3 Democrats and no Republicans; (c) middle: followed 1-3 Democrats and 1-3 Republicans; (d) far-middle: followed 4-6 Democrats and 4-6 Republicans; (e) right: follow no Democrats and 1-3 Republicans; (f) far-right: followed no Democrats and 4-6 Republicans.
elements.sent-2411-322: We started with members of Congress since their political affiliations are known and are often used to estimate Twitter users’ political leaning~\cite{barbera2015tweeting, barbera2015birds .}
elements.sent-2411-323: Next, we removed follower accounts that are likely to be bots.
elements.sent-2411-324: We used the Botometer API and filtered out accounts with Complete Automation Probability above 0.6~\cite{yang2022botometer .}
elements.sent-2411-325: Finally, we took a stratified sample of 300 accounts, selecting 50 users from each of the six groups defined above.
elements.sent-2411-326: Next, for each of the selected users, we collected all of the accounts they followed and collected all the posts by those users as far back as we could.
elements.sent-2411-327: Then, to replicate the reverse chronological feed of each user, we merged all posts by the accounts they followed and sorted from most to least recent.
elements.sent-2411-328: We retain only the English posts that would have appeared in their reverse chronological feeds in the previous two weeks.
elements.sent-2411-329: Finally, we merged the two-week feeds of all users, removed duplicate posts, and took a random sample of 50,000 posts.
elements.sent-2411-330: We later randomly split this dataset into 45,000 posts for training and 5,000 posts for testing.
elements.sent-2411-331: We annotated all 50,000 posts by prompting GPT-4 using a definition proposed by the Pew Research Center~\cite{bestvater2022politics :.}
elements.verbatim-2411-001: Political content on Twitter is varied and can be about officials and activists, social issues, or news and current events. Looking at the following tweet, would you categorize it as POLITICAL or NOT POLITICAL content? Answer 1 if it is POLITICAL, 0 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-332: This step annotated 34.2\% of the posts as political and 65.8\% as non-political.
elements.sent-2411-333: We used this dataset to fine-tune a pre-trained RoBERTa model (roberta-base).
elements.sent-2411-334: We evaluate the model on a held-out dataset composed of 5,000 posts annotated by GPT-4 and a random subset of 300 posts annotated by humans.
elements.sent-2411-335: For each post, we collect three labels using Prolific and assign the value based on majority voting.
elements.sent-2411-336: The model evaluated on these testing sets achieves an F1 score of 93.6\% on the GPT-4 labels and an F1 score of 91.3\% on the human ground truth.

elements.sent-2411-337: The scoring service is a Java backend responsible for returning the number of each political post's eight problematic factors.
elements.sent-2411-338: It is designed to minimize latency.
elements.sent-2411-339: When a new request is received, it proceeds as follows: after the political classifier identifies the posts to score, they are sent to this service in one request as a list of textual items.
elements.sent-2411-340: This service first splits the list into chunks of at most ten items and then compiles eight prompts for each of them.
elements.sent-2411-341: The prompts are in the format:.
elements.verbatim-2411-002: Do the following messages express partisan animosity? Partisan animosity is defined as "dislike for opposing partisans". FORMAT: The input messages are given as JSON lines in the format {"id": <message_id>, "message": <message>}. The output must be a JSON array of objects in the format [{"id": <message_id>, "answer": <YES or NO>}, ... ]. INPUT MESSAGES: ...
elements.sent-2411-342: The prompt starts with the question and the definition of the factor as described in Table 1, specifies the input and output format, and ends with the list of posts encoded in JSON objects, one for each line.
elements.sent-2411-343: The posts are grouped with at most ten items per prompt as a tradeoff between making too many API calls and keeping the response time low.
elements.sent-2411-344: This implementation ensures that a feed containing ten political posts results in 8 requests to OpenAI instead of 80.
elements.sent-2411-345: These requests run in parallel using a custom connection pool that shares HTTP connections and iterates round-robin on multiple API tokens.
elements.sent-2411-346: Each request takes a few seconds to receive a response, but each connection has a maximum waiting time of 8 seconds to ensure a slow API call does not disrupt the user experience.
elements.sent-2411-347: When the timeout is reached, the event is logged, and the factor is set as non-present.
elements.sent-2411-348: To ensure a fast response from OpenAI, we use GPT-3.5 Turbo, which offers a good tradeoff between speed and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-349: We use a fixed seed for reproducibility purposes.
elements.sent-2411-350: Since many popular posts appear in multiple feeds, to avoid scoring multiple times the same text, each post score is cached in a backend database and reused when necessary without including the post in the request to OpenAI.
elements.figure-2411-014: \begin{tabular}{p{0.4\textwidth} p{0.6\textwidth}} Variable Definition Partisan Animosity Do the following messages express partisan animosity? Partisan animosity is defined as "dislike for opposing partisans". Support for Undemocratic Practices Do the following messages express support for undemocratic practices? Support for undemocratic practices is defined as "willingness to forgo democratic principles for partisan gain". Undemocratic practices are undemocratic tendencies or actions such as reducing polling stations in areas that support their opponents, attacking the independence of the judiciary, undermining the free press, challenging the legitimacy of election results, or encouraging political violence. Support for Partisan Violence Do the following messages express support for partisan violence? Support for partisan violence is defined as a "willingness to use violent tactics against outpartisans". Examples of partisan violence include sending threatening and intimidating messages to the opponent party, harassing the opponent party on the Internet, using violence in advancing their political goals or winning more races in the next election. Support for Undemocratic Candidates Do the following messages express support for undemocratic candidates? Support for undemocratic candidates is defined as "willingness to ignore democratic practices to elect inparty candidates." Undemocratic candidates often support undemocratic practices such as reducing polling stations in areas that support their opponents, attacking the independence of the judiciary, undermining the free press, challenging the legitimacy of election results, or encouraging political violence. Opposition to Bipartisan Cooperation Do the following messages express opposition to bipartisanship? Opposition to bipartisanship is defined as "resistance to cross-partisan collaboration". Social Distrust Do the following messages express social distrust? Social distrust is defined as "distrust of people in general". Social Distance Do the following messages express social distance? Social distance is defined as "resistance to interpersonal contact with outpartisans". Messages that increase social distance may contain terms that increase distrust, distance, insecurity, hate, prejudice, or discrimination. Biased Evaluation of Politicized Facts Do the following messages express a biased evaluation of politicized facts? Biased evaluation of politicized facts is defined as "skepticism of facts that favor the worldview of the other party". Messages supporting a biased evaluation of politicized facts may partially present political facts or discuss a controversial issue with a certain political stance. \end{tabular} \caption{Definition of the eight factors used in the GPT prompts.} \label{tab:prompts}
elements.sent-2411-351: To enroll in the experiment, participants had to take a screening task by installing a browser extension.
elements.sent-2411-352: The extension opened X, asked participants to log in if necessary, scrolled through their feed automatically, and verified that at least 5\% of the posts in their ``For You'' feed were about politics or social issues.
elements.sent-2411-353: Around 73\% of the participants qualified and were invited to the full study.

elements.sent-2411-358: For the two groups (experiments), we independently implement an FDR adjustment~\cite{anderson2008multiple , grouping the variables as follows:.}
elements.sent-2411-359: We also use correct for multiple comparisons when examining heterogeneous treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-360: Primary tests of heterogeneity: We used sharpened FDR-adjusted p-values with $L_x * K_x$ hypothesis tests, where $L_x$ is the number of primary moderators for the corresponding group, and $K_x$ is the number of outcomes in the corresponding group (i.e., $K_1$, $K_2$, or $K_3$ above).
elements.sent-2411-361: Secondary tests of heterogeneity: We specify the secondary moderators that we considered for each group of outcomes (primary, secondary, tertiary).
elements.sent-2411-362: We used sharpened FDR-adjusted p-values with $(L_x + L_y) * K_x$ hypothesis tests, where $L_x$ is the number of primary and $L_y$ is the number of secondary moderators for the corresponding group, and $K_x$ is the number of outcomes in the corresponding group (i.e., $K_1$, $K_2$, or $K_3$ above).
elements.sent-2411-363: Auxiliary tests: Not adjusted, generally these are variables for which statistical significance should not matter.
elements.sent-2411-364: The preregistered regression analysis for the in-feed effect is reported in \Tabref{table:affective_polarization_infeed_regressions , and the regression analyses for the post-experiment effect are reported in \Tabref{table:affective_polarization_prepost_regressions .}}
elements.sent-2411-365: For both experiments, the treatment effect remains statistically significant.
elements.sent-2411-366: We note that in this sample there is also no evidence of covariate imbalance (reduce, post-experiment: $p=0.86$; reduce, in-feed: $p=0.59$; increase, post-experiment: $p=0.77$; increase, in-feed: $p = 0.18$), asymmetric attrition rates (reduce, post-experiment: $p=0.55$; reduce, in-feed: $p=0.28$; increase, post-experiment: $p=0.4$; increase, in-feed: $p=0.56$), or asymmetric attrition patterns (reduce, post-experiment: $p = 0.49$; reduce, in-feed: $p = 0.69$; increase, post-experiment: $p = 0.78$; increase, in-feed: $p = 0.98$).

elements.figure-2411-033: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{images/factors_contribution_individual_regressions.pdf} \caption{Each value represents the estimated effect from a separate regression model. The dependent variable is the political feeling response in the post-experiment survey, controlling for both the pre-experiment survey response and the fraction of political posts viewed over a ten-day period for at least one second, which expressed support for a particular factor (i.e., $post\_experiment\_rating \sim pre\_experiment\_rating + fraction\_v_n$, where $v_n$ is a specific factor such as partisan animosity or social distrust). All participants are included in the analysis (N=1,256).}\label{fig:factors_contribution}
elements.sent-2411-374: During the baseline period, we observed that political posts enjoyed a higher reshare rate: 0.677\%, compared to 0.484\% for non-political posts (a relative increase of 39.7\%; U=714,022; P$=$0.046).
elements.sent-2411-375: Similarly, political content had a higher favorite rate, with an average engagement rate for posts viewed for at least one second of 5.07\% vs. 4.29\% for non-political posts (U=645,266; P$=$0.001).
elements.sent-2411-376: We observed that AAPA posts, while shared more frequently than non-AAPA content, did not exceed political non-AAPA posts in share rate (0.662\% vs. 0.674\%; U=668,945, P=0.003).
elements.sent-2411-377: Similarly, although AAPA content had a higher favorite rate than non-AAPA posts, it was favorited less than non-AAPA political posts (4.6\% vs. 5.23\%; U=711,396, P$<$0.001).
elements.sent-2411-378: When examining the effects of our interventions, none of the differences reached statistical significance.
elements.sent-2411-379: In the Increased Exposure experiment, the average number of daily sessions per participant--defined as scrolling activities separated by at least one hour--was 1.65 in the control group and 1.53 in the treatment group (U=32{, 220; P=0.18).}
elements.sent-2411-380: In the Reduced Exposure experiment, the control and treatment groups averaged 1.53 and 1.49 sessions per day, respectively (U=66{, 940; P=0.71).}
elements.sent-2411-381: After controlling for the average daily sessions during the baseline period and the recruitment platform, the change in session count was not statistically significant in either experiment (Increased Exposure: -0.07 sessions, CI [-0.19, 0.04]; P=0.197; 95\% Reduced Exposure: -0.04 sessions, CI [-0.13, 0.05] P=0.381; 95\%).
elements.sent-2411-382: The differences in the time spent on the platform across conditions also do not reach statistical significance.
elements.sent-2411-383: In the Increased Exposure experiment, participants in the control condition spent on average of 44.4 minutes vs. 41.3 minutes in the treatment condition (U=37,545; P=0.12), while in the Reduced Exposure, they spent 46.8 minutes in the control and 42.2 minutes in the treatment condition (U=70,678; P=0.10).
elements.sent-2411-384: Similarly, repost rates during the experimental period did not differ significantly between treatment and control groups.
elements.sent-2411-385: In the Increased Exposure experiment, the repost rate was 0.52\% in the control group and 1.07\% in the treatment group (U=30{, 859; P=0.83).}
elements.sent-2411-386: In the Reduced Exposure experiment, the repost rates were 0.74\% for the control group and 0.46\% for the treatment group.
elements.sent-2411-387: When controlling for user baseline averages and the platform using regression analysis (\Figref{fig:retweet ), the changes in repost rates remained non-significant (Increased Exposure: increase of 0.27\%, 95\% CI [-0.002, 0.008]; P=0.29; Reduced Exposure: reduction of -0.06\%, 95\% CI [-0.002, 0.001] P=0.503;).}
elements.sent-2411-388: The favorite rates during the experimental period also showed no significant differences.
elements.sent-2411-389: The overall favorite rate during the baseline was 4.73\%.
elements.sent-2411-390: Controlling for user baseline and platform (\Figref{fig:favorite ), the favorite rate changed by 0.14\% in the Increased Exposure group (95\% CI [-0.009, 0.012]; P=0.796) and decreased by 0.29\% in the Reduced Exposure group (95\% CI [-0.010, 0.004]; P=0.43).}
elements.sent-2411-391: Finally, we observed no significant differences between treatment and control groups in terms of the number of new posts or replies during the experimental period.

elements.subsection-2411-022: Qualitative feedback on the impact of the experiment
elements.sent-2411-396: At the end of the study, in the post-survey, we asked participants: ``Did the Chrome extension affect your experience on Twitter/X in any way?" This question was open-ended, and 1,218 participants (96.9\%) provided a non-empty response.
elements.sent-2411-397: Most answers were short and indicated no impact, such as ``No" or ``Not that I could tell." We individually coded each response with the support of AI (GPT-4), providing the original survey question for context and asking whether the participant reported any impact on their experience on Twitter/X.
elements.sent-2411-398: The majority of participants (overall: 74.2\%; increase, control: 73.7\%; increase, treatment: 72.9\%; reduce, control: 76.4\%; reduce, treatment: 73.4\%) explicitly stated that they did not notice any impact on their experience.
elements.sent-2411-399: We manually reviewed a random sample of 100 responses and found complete agreement with GPT-4's annotations.
elements.sent-2411-400: Among the remaining responses, we manually coded 10.3\% as reporting a ``positive experience," often for reasons unrelated to the extension, such as: ``Twitter seemed better organized," ``It made me feel informed," and ``It helped me explore the social network better." Additionally, 4.1\% reported observing more political content; however, this observation was uniformly distributed across conditions, likely reflecting an overall increase in political content during the election period.
elements.sent-2411-401: Finally, 2.8\% of participants mentioned noticing a slowdown in the website's performance.
elements.sent-2411-402: The remaining answers mention details unrelated to the assigned condition, such as ``It made me use it on my laptop more, which was a bit annoying" or ``Having to use the For You page I noticed a lot of tweets about AI." To investigate this further, we adopted a quantitative approach by embedding participant responses using a pre-trained BERT-based model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2).
elements.sent-2411-403: We then predicted the assigned experiment using logistic regression with L2 regularization, employing 5-fold cross-validation.
elements.sent-2411-404: The classification achieved an F1 score of 53.9\% ($\pm$ 4.7\%) on a balanced dataset.
elements.sent-2411-405: Similarly, we predicted the experimental condition (treatment or control) for each experiment.
elements.sent-2411-406: For the reduced experiment, the F1 score was 47.4\% ($\pm$ 6.8\%), and for the increased experiment, it was 50.5\% ($\pm$ 4.7\%).
elements.sent-2411-407: These results suggest that the experimental condition did not significantly impact participants' experience, as they did not perceive or report notable differences.. \end{appendices.}
